ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for commercial isotonic beverage product; SNOMED CT coverage for branded sports drinks is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 325 ml can of 100 Plus, the typical single-serve format sold in Singapore and Malaysia convenience stores and hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Carbonated Sports Drink / Isotonic Beverage (SR Legacy / Branded Foods reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for 100 Plus are adapted from USDA FoodData Central branded and SR Legacy entries for carbonated isotonic sports drinks. The per-100g values (energy 40 kcal, carbohydrate 10 g, sugar 10 g, sodium 49 mg) are consistent with the declared nutrition panel on 100 Plus cans and align with USDA FoodData Central isotonic beverage references. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a carbohydrate-electrolyte beverage with no protein or fat ingredients.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values — glucose-based sugar solutions and carbonated isotonic sports drinks
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 100 Plus in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 73 is estimated from the closest analogous food: glucose-sucrose mixed sugar solution in water at equivalent concentration (~10g/100ml), consistent with published GI values for glucose (GI=100) and sucrose (GI=65) blended at approximately equal proportions, yielding a weighted average of ~73. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (73 × 32.5) / 100 = 23.7. Serving carbohydrate = 10g/100g × 325g = 32.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10g sugar per 100ml; AHA 2018 SSB threshold exceeded
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid 100 Plus as its 10g/100ml sugar content exceeds the AHA 2018 SSB threshold of 5g/100ml, promoting insulin-driven sodium retention and weight gain that independently elevates blood pressure regardless of the beverage's relatively low sodium content of 49mg/100ml.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (73) sugar-sweetened beverage; 10g rapidly absorbed glucose and sucrose per 100ml with zero dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid 100 Plus entirely, as its 10g/100ml of rapidly absorbed glucose and sucrose (GI ~73, GL 23.7 per can) causes acute postprandial blood glucose spikes with no fibre to attenuate absorption; the sugar-free variant (100 Plus Zero) is the recommended alternative per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages associated with increased CVD risk; zero saturated fat and low sodium are mitigating factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid 100 Plus because regular SSB consumption is independently associated with increased CVD risk and triglyceride elevation per AHA guidelines, though the absence of saturated fat and trans fat and the low sodium content (49mg/100ml) mean the risk is primarily driven by the sugar load rather than lipid or sodium factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 100 Plus is a carbonated isotonic sports drink manufactured by Fraser & Neave (F&N) and originally developed in Singapore in 1983. It is one of the most iconic and widely consumed beverages in Singapore and Malaysia, commonly served at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and restaurants as a standard meal accompaniment. The drink is lightly carbonated and formulated to replenish electrolytes — primarily sodium and potassium — lost during physical activity or in hot tropical climates. Its flavour profile is mildly citrus-sweet with a clean, refreshing finish and gentle effervescence. Nutritionally, 100 Plus provides 40 kcal per 100ml, derived entirely from 10g of carbohydrates (glucose and sucrose blend) with zero protein and zero fat by product formulation. Sodium content is approximately 49mg per 100ml, contributing to its isotonic electrolyte profile. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 73 (high), making it a high-glycemic beverage unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. A 325ml standard can delivers approximately 130 kcal and 32.5g of sugar, representing a significant glycemic load of 23.7. The product is halal-certified, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. A zero-sugar variant (100 Plus Zero) using sucralose and acesulfame K is available for health-conscious consumers. 100 Plus is culturally embedded in Singaporean and Malaysian food culture and is frequently associated with Nasi Lemak, Curry Rice, and other local meal occasions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of 100 Plus?
What is the sugar content of 100 Plus?
Can I drink 100 Plus if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of 100 Plus?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with the declared nutrition information panel on 100 Plus product packaging and align with USDA FoodData Central isotonic beverage references. Core macros (energy, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium) are confirmed by product label data cross-referenced against USDA branded food entries. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from approved University of Sydney GI Database methodology for glucose-sucrose solutions at equivalent concentration.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as 100 Plus is a carbohydrate-electrolyte beverage containing no protein ingredients by formulation. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central isotonic beverage reference. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by product formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — no fibre-containing ingredients present. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero by product formulation. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient data beyond sodium, potassium, and phosphorus not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific isotonic beverage formulation as of 2024. potassium_mg (11.0 mg/100ml) and phosphorus_mg (11.0 mg/100ml) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central isotonic sports drink reference entries consistent with potassium phosphate electrolyte content. water_g estimated at 89.0g/100g based on mass balance (100 - 10g carbohydrate - 1g electrolytes/additives). Portions deduplicated: input contained three identical portion entries; consolidated to one default portion of 325g (1 can). Cultural ethnic_tags 'Curry Rice' and 'Nasi Lemak' retained as meal-pairing context tags rather than cuisine identifiers. GI of 73 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for 100 Plus exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimation methodology documented in gi_source.
